original_abstract:
Background: Medical image segmentation is a foundational step in image processing, and the ability to accurately compare segmented images is vital for measuring research progress. However, evaluating medical segmentation presents several significant challenges: the difficulty of selecting appropriate metrics from a diverse pool; the prevalence of inconsistent metric definitions in existing literature; the computational inefficiency of many implementations when processing large 3D volumes; and a general lack of support for fuzzy (probabilistic) segmentations within standard evaluation frameworks.

Results: We provide a comprehensive overview of 20 evaluation metrics selected through an extensive literature review. To support fuzzy segmentation—which characterizes the membership level of voxels across multiple classes—we provide fuzzy mathematical definitions for all 20 metrics. We also analyze the specific properties of these metrics to serve as a practical guide for researchers in selecting the most appropriate evaluation tools for their specific tasks. Finally, we introduce an advanced, open-source evaluation tool that implements these metrics using highly optimized algorithms. This tool is designed to maintain high performance in terms of speed and memory efficiency even when processing massive datasets, such as whole-body MRI or CT volumes.

Conclusion: This work provides both a high-performance evaluation tool for 3D medical image segmentation and a set of formal guidelines to help researchers choose a statistically sound subset of metrics tailored to their data and clinical objectives.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract Medical image segmentation is one of the fundamental tasks in image processing and comparing the quality of the resulting segmentations is crucial to quantify the advance in research. Medical segmentation evaluation is a challenging task due to the large number of metrics to choose from, ambiguities in the metrics definitions reported in the literature, long computation times for algorithms evaluated on 3D images, and that most current evaluation tools do not handle fuzzy segmentations.

Conclusion: In this paper we provide an overview of the 20 evaluation criteria chosen from a large number of criteria mentioned in the literature. For purposes of fuzzy segmentation, in which voxel membership for each class is expressed by a degree (a value belonging to the interval [0, 1]), we give fuzzy mathematical definitions of all 20 criteria proposed in the literature. Moreover, characteristics of these criteria are given in order to facilitate the work of the researchers searching for the evaluation criteria more suitable for the task they solve. An advanced open-source tool evaluating performance of the segmentation results with chosen criteria has been implemented. It uses an optimized version of algorithms employed for the evaluation of medical images’ segmentation results. The tool enables fast and memory-efficient processing of huge volumes of images such as full body MRI or CT scans.

Conclusion: This paper provides an efficient assessment tool for 3D medical image segmentation and also provides recommendations for selecting a statistically relevant subset of measures from the evaluation metrics given the characteristics of the dataset and clinical needs.
